stories.title: Texas Wins 2026 WCWS Title, Sweeps Texas Tech 4-1 in Game 2

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Texas wins. Back-to-back national champions. 🥎

Longhorns 4, Texas Tech 1 in Game 2.

Kayden Henry HR. Kavan closes it out with 5 Ks.

Sweep. Dynasty. The best team in college softball. 🤠

#HookEm #WCWS

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Back-to-back national titles. Texas has done it.

The Longhorns swept Texas Tech in the WCWS Championship Series — 7-3, 4-1.

Fifth program in history to win consecutive Women's College World Series titles. 🥎

#HookEm #WCWS

facebook.long_form:
Texas is the national champion — again.

The Longhorns defeated Texas Tech 4-1 in Game 2 of the WCWS Championship Series last night, sweeping the series and claiming their second consecutive Women's College World Series title. It wasn't easy, and it wasn't always pretty, but Texas found a way — just like they did in 2025.

Texas Tech took the lead first. The Red Raiders scored in the third inning and held a 1-0 advantage into the middle innings. But in the fifth, the Longhorns chipped away with two runs on a Texas Tech throwing error, taking a 2-1 lead they would not give back. Kayden Henry put it out of reach in the seventh with an opposite-field home run, and Texas added another run for the 4-1 final.

Coach Mike White made a gutsy call to start Citlaly Gutierrez instead of ace Teagan Kavan. The gamble worked. Gutierrez went three innings, freshman Hannah Wells bridged, and Kavan came in for the final two innings — striking out five of the last six batters she faced.

Two championships. Two sweeps in the finals. Texas softball is not playing around.

Who was your MVP of the 2026 WCWS? Drop your pick below! ⬇️
facebook.image_caption: Texas 4, Texas Tech 1. Back-to-back national champions. The dynasty is real. 🥎 #HookEm #WCWS

stories.title: Teagan Kavan Becomes First Two-Time MOP in WCWS History

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Teagan Kavan is the first two-time Most Outstanding Player in WCWS history.

No one has ever done it twice. She just did it back-to-back.

2026 WCWS: 4-1, 1.47 ERA, 30 Ks in 33 innings. Flawless. 🔐

#HookEm #WCWS

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Kavan's 2026 WCWS numbers: 4-1, 1.47 ERA, 30 Ks.

She closed out the championship with 5 strikeouts in 2 innings.

First pitcher in history to win the WCWS MOP award twice. 🔐

#HookEm #WCWS

facebook.long_form:
Teagan Kavan made history last night — again.

For the second consecutive year, the Texas ace was named Most Outstanding Player of the Women's College World Series. That has never happened before. Not once in the tournament's history. Kavan is the first pitcher ever to earn back-to-back WCWS MOP honors.

Her 2026 tournament numbers tell the story: 4-1 record, two saves, 1.47 ERA, and 30 strikeouts in 33.1 innings pitched. She threw a complete game in Game 1 of the championship series, allowing three hits in a 7-3 win. In Game 2, she came in relief in the final two innings and struck out five batters to seal the title.

What made Thursday night even more remarkable is that her coach held her back — starting Citlaly Gutierrez to protect Kavan's arm — and she responded with a shutdown performance when the moment demanded it most.

Kavan arrived in Austin as one of the most hyped pitchers in the country. She has exceeded every expectation twice over. Two national championships. Two MOP awards. She is, without question, one of the greatest to ever take the circle at the Women's College World Series.

Here's to you, Teagan. 🔐
facebook.image_caption: First pitcher in WCWS history to win MOP back-to-back. Teagan Kavan is in a class by herself. 🔐 #HookEm #WCWS

stories.title: Texas Is the Fifth Program in History to Win Back-to-Back WCWS Titles

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Texas is now one of five programs in history to win back-to-back Women's College World Series titles.

That list is short for a reason. What the Longhorns are doing is rare.

#HookEm #WCWS #CollegeSoftball

facebook.long_form:
Back-to-back national championships. Say it out loud. That is one of the rarest things in college softball.

Texas is now one of only five programs in the entire history of the Women's College World Series to win consecutive national titles. The list is short because it is so incredibly hard to do. Champions lose key players to graduation, to the AUSL, to fatigue. Opponents study you for a full year. The pressure to repeat is immense.

Texas did it anyway.

This Longhorn program — built by coach Mike White, anchored by Teagan Kavan on the mound and Katie Stewart and Kayden Henry in the lineup — swept Texas Tech in the 2026 championship series. The Red Raiders brought everything they had: NiJaree Canady, a No. 11 seed with a 63-10 record, two victories over No. 1 Alabama. And they still couldn't dethrone the reigning champions.

The five programs to win back-to-back WCWS titles represent the absolute elite of college softball history. Texas joined that list last night.

What does it take to build something like this? Depth. Pitching. Clutch players in clutch moments. Mike White has all three.

The dynasty conversation is now officially open. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: Fifth program in WCWS history to win back-to-back. Texas belongs in the all-time elite. 🥎 #HookEm #WCWS #CollegeSoftball

stories.title: NiJaree Canady's College Career Ends — A Legacy Without a Ring

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
NiJaree Canady's college career is over.

NCAA-record 19 WCWS games. 100+ wins. 1,000+ strikeouts.

She didn't get the title — but she gave everything she had every time she took the circle. 🥎

#WreckEm #WCWS

facebook.long_form:
NiJaree Canady's college career is over, and the softball world is better for having watched every pitch.

Canady appeared in an NCAA-record 19 Women's College World Series games across her career. She transferred from Stanford to Texas Tech with one goal: win a national championship. She won over 100 games and struck out more than 1,000 batters in her college career. She is a two-time Honda Sport Award winner and multiple-time NFCA Pitcher of the Year. She was the first player to receive a golden ticket in the 2026 AUSL Draft.

And yet, the ring that she chased for five years will belong to someone else.

Texas Tech gave her every chance. The Red Raiders — a No. 11 seed who knocked out No. 1 Alabama twice — were a worthy opponent for the game's best team. Canady was brilliant throughout this tournament. She pitched a shutout to eliminate Alabama. She carried her team to the championship series.

It just wasn't enough to beat Teagan Kavan and the Longhorns.

There is no shame in that ending. Canady's legacy isn't defined by one ring she didn't win — it's defined by the thousands of strikeouts, the shutouts in the biggest moments, and the standard she set for what a pitcher in this sport can be.

The AUSL's Texas Volts are getting one of the greatest pitchers to ever play college softball. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: 100+ wins. 1,000+ Ks. NCAA-record 19 WCWS games. NiJaree Canady gave everything. 🥎 #WreckEm #WCWS

stories.title: The Texas Dynasty Is Real — What Two Titles in a Row Actually Means

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Katie Stewart. Kayden Henry. Teagan Kavan. Coach Mike White.

This isn't a one-year wonder. Texas softball has built something special in Austin.

Back-to-back. 🤠

#HookEm #WCWS #CollegeSoftball

facebook.long_form:
You can say dynasty now.

Two consecutive Women's College World Series championships. Two sweeps in the championship series. Multiple program records shattered. A coaching staff that seems to always have the right answer in the biggest moments.

Texas softball is the best program in the country, and they have two titles in a row to prove it.

Teagan Kavan will be remembered as one of the greatest pitchers in college softball history — two-time MOP, two titles, program records that may stand for decades. Katie Stewart made WCWS history with her bat. Kayden Henry hit the home run that put Game 2 out of reach. Coach Mike White outmaneuvered his opponent with a starting pitching gamble that paid off beautifully.

This team is more than just talented. They are clutch. They are deep. They are the standard every other program is chasing.

Congratulations to the 2026 national champions. Texas Longhorns. Back-to-back. 🤠🥎
facebook.image_caption: Back-to-back. The Texas dynasty is real. 🤠 #HookEm #WCWS #CollegeSoftball
